article_title: MIT and Harvard Teach Language Models to Ask Better Questions, Lifting a Small Model's Battleship Win Rate From 8% to 82%

findings.message: Source fetched via Archive.org fallback (original returned 200)

content_preview.title: MIT and Harvard Teach Language Models to Ask Better Questions, Lifting a Small Model's Battleship Win Rate From 8% to 82%
content_preview.summary: An ICLR paper from MIT CSAIL and Harvard shows Monte Carlo inference helps Llama 4 Scout outpace GPT-5 at a Battleship test bed for around 1% of its cost.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Researchers at MIT and Harvard have shown that giving language models a structured way to reason about what they don't yet know can dramatically improve their ability to ask useful questions. In a study presented as an oral presentation at the International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR) in April, the team used a collaborative version of the board game Battleship as a test bed and found that a small model equipped with their inference method could outpace a frontier m...

editor_notes.content_quality: Strong research News piece (747 words) that lays out the method, the per-model results, and the limitations, with disciplined per-outlet quote attribution.
editor_notes.source_verification: Both snapshots read from disk (HTTP 200; source-1 is an archive capture of the Tech Xplore page, which is the committed provenance copy). source-0 (MIT News) confirms verbatim every quote — Grand ('Today's language models are primarily optimized to answer complex queries' / 'Our work shows that asking informative questions depends on the ability to predict and simulate the world'), Pepe ('GPT-5 can beat your average Battleship player …'), Andreas ('What I find most exciting about this work …'), and Stanford's Robert Hawkins ('makes a compelling case that the real bottleneck …') — plus the 'individual particles' Monte Carlo description, the 8%→82% Llama 4 Scout result, the GPT-5 at ~1% cost claim, the 15% auto-formalization boost, the Guess Who? figures (Llama 4 Scout 30%→over 72%, GPT-4o 62%→90%), the ICLR oral presentation, and the author list (Grand, Pepe, Andreas, Tenenbaum). source-1 (Tech Xplore) confirms the 'Collaborative Battleship' captain/spotter setup, the 8%→82% headline figure (supporting the one compound citation), and the GPT-4o-mini 'nearly 30%' / Claude 4 Opus 'about eight points' answering results.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Every quote is verbatim with correct speaker attribution; every model name and percentage traces to a cited source. The sole 'both MIT News and Tech Xplore' compound citation (8%→82%) is carried by both outlets. Tenenbaum and the MIT-only quotes are correctly cited to MIT News alone; the Tech-Xplore-only answering figures (GPT-4o-mini, Claude 4 Opus) are cited to Tech Xplore alone — no overreach.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral; the 'What We Don't Know' section honestly scopes the board-game setting, the still-unbeaten expert humans, and the open question of transfer to real-world agent tasks.
editor_notes.originality: New topic — no prior Battleship / question-asking coverage in the archive. The contributor correctly disambiguated this ICLR Collaborative Battleship work from unrelated similarly-named papers.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Fully-verified AI-research News piece with verbatim quotes and correct per-outlet attribution. APPROVE.
